summary: McKenzie shares his inspiration from Brian Plexico's story of releasing a profitable software product in just seven days. He argues that the key to microISV success is finding an unfilled niche and targeting it aggressively. If you're the sole provider of software that solves a genuine pain point, people will pay regardless of how trivial the problem seems to outsiders. He notes that the world is full of niches that have never attracted someone with basic programming skills.
key_points: A profitable software product can be conceived and released in as little as seven days if the niche is right
Success requires finding an unfilled niche and targeting it aggressively — being the sole provider for a real pain point
The perceived triviality of a problem to outsiders is irrelevant if the people experiencing it are willing to pay
Competition is a later-stage concern; the initial opportunity lies in the vast number of niches no programmer has bothered to serve
money_quotes: If you are the sole source in the world for software to fill your need, and there are people who are actually hurt by that need, then people will be happy to pay you money to ease it for them.
It doesn't matter if the need sounds trivial to people who don't feel it, or whether another developer could do the same thing in their lunch break.
Its a wide, wide world out there and there are an embarassment of niches which have never caught the eye of somebody with rudimentary programming expertise.
I think the key here is a) finding an unfilled niche and b) targetting it aggressively.
key_insight: The world is full of profitable software niches too small or obscure to attract attention, and serving even one of them as the sole provider is enough to build a business.
